findings.description: Title slide layout is not preserved.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, page 1 has a centered title block with the NC logo on the left, title and presenter details aligned across the middle band, and banner images at top and bottom. In the HTML, the elements are stacked vertically with large whitespace; the title appears lower on the page and the original centered composition is lost.

findings.description: Reading order on the title slide differs from the PDF.
findings.evidence: The PDF presents the top banner, then logo/title/presenter information together, then the bottom banner. In the HTML, the top banner, logo, bottom banner, and then the title are encountered in a different sequence.

In the PDF, page 2 has a centered heading near the top, a left-side bullet list, and an image placed to the right of the bullets. In the HTML, the title and metadata from page 1 appear above the page 2 section, and the page 2 image is moved below the bullets rather than beside them.

findings.description: Image placement on page 2 is altered.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows the construction image to the right of the bullet list; the HTML places the same image below the list, changing the visual relationship between text and image.

findings.description: Page 2 reading flow is affected by content carryover and reordering.
findings.evidence: The HTML shows page 1 title/author information before the page 2 heading, whereas the PDF page 2 begins directly with 'DEMLR Dam Safety Rulemaking' followed by bullets and image.

findings.description: Page 3 has reduced visual emphasis and spacing compared with the PDF.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses a prominent centered blue italic heading and large bullet text with spacious layout. The HTML keeps the content but compresses it into a denser text block with less separation.

findings.description: Heading and body formatting on page 3 are not fully preserved.
findings.evidence: The PDF clearly differentiates the heading with large blue italic styling and enlarged bullet text. In the HTML, the heading is plainer and the body text is smaller and less visually distinct.

findings.description: Page 4 layout is more condensed than the PDF.
findings.evidence: The PDF presents a large centered heading and oversized introductory sentence with bullets beneath. The HTML preserves the sequence but compresses the text into a smaller block at the top of the page.
The PDF uses large display text and strong bullet formatting. In the HTML, the same content appears with substantially smaller text and less visual hierarchy.

findings.description: Page 5 heading and list block are compressed.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows a prominent title and a large, easy-to-scan list occupying the main page area. The HTML keeps the list but presents it in a tighter, less spacious layout.

findings.description: Page 5 formatting emphasis is partially lost.
findings.evidence: Underlined/bold emphasis remains in the HTML, but the PDF’s larger title and body text create stronger visual distinction than the smaller HTML rendering.

findings.description: Page 6 heading and body text styling are less prominent in HTML.
findings.evidence: The PDF page uses a large blue italic heading and large bold rule title with a clearly separated bullet list. The HTML preserves the structure and text but reduces font size and visual separation.

findings.description: Page 7 regulatory impact table is converted to plain aligned text rather than a visible table.
findings.evidence: The PDF presents the impact analysis as two aligned text columns without visible grid lines, while the HTML renders it as a bordered table with the same rows and values. Structure is intact, but presentation differs.

findings.description: Page 8 content is split differently across the page.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows the 'Questions and Answers' heading with contact information laid out as paragraphs and aligned lines on one page. The HTML moves part of the contact information into a table lower on the page, creating a more segmented layout.

findings.description: Page 8 contact list is transformed into a table.
findings.evidence: The PDF lists names, emails, and phone numbers as aligned text lines under 'Questions about the rule can be directed to'. The HTML renders this information in a 3-column table. Data appears preserved, but the original presentation is changed.
